questions.question: What is the first step in answering an exactly-once design question well?
options: Define the boundary of the guarantee before claiming it.
Promise global end-to-end exactly-once semantics immediately.
Assume the broker alone solves all duplicates.
Avoid discussing failures so the answer stays simple.
questions.explanation: Scope is the core of an honest answer.

questions.question: Why does idempotency still matter even when a platform advertises strong delivery semantics?
options: Because retries, replays, and side-effect ambiguity still happen operationally.
Because idempotency is only for batch systems.
Because strong semantics remove the need for acknowledgements.
Because it guarantees external APIs never time out.
questions.explanation: Operational duplicates and ambiguity do not disappear.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals whether an exactly-once claim is working in practice?
options: Duplicate-attempt rate and dedupe-hit rate.
Average service logo size.
The number of interview prep files in the repo.
Median source line length in consumer code.
questions.explanation: You need to see duplicate pressure and suppression behavior.

questions.question: Why is 'exactly once into our database' usually easier to defend than 'exactly once end to end'?
options: Because the database boundary is controllable, while external side effects add more ambiguity.
Because databases never fail or retry.
Because end-to-end systems do not need acknowledgements.
Because all external APIs support distributed transactions.
questions.explanation: Controllable boundaries are where stronger guarantees are realistic.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with a longer dedupe-retention window?
options: You catch more delayed duplicates but spend more on storage and lookup state.
You eliminate the need for replay controls.
You guarantee all downstream side effects are unique forever.
You remove all transaction complexity.
questions.explanation: Longer duplicate protection has real cost.

questions.question: Which failure is most directly mitigated by an outbox pattern?
options: A consumer updating state successfully but failing before publishing a dependent event.
A topic being created with too many partitions.
A scheduler clock drifting by one second.
A metrics query overloading a dashboard.
questions.explanation: Outbox patterns align local state changes with downstream publication.

questions.question: Why is replay dangerous if it bypasses the normal dedupe path?
options: Because historical recovery traffic can then recreate duplicates the system would normally suppress.
Because replayed events become unreadable.
Because acknowledgements stop working after replay.
Because replay only matters for logs, not messages.
questions.explanation: Recovery paths must preserve the same correctness controls.

questions.question: What is the strongest senior-level answer when an interviewer insists on exactly-once wording?
options: State a bounded guarantee clearly, explain the mechanism, and name the residual risk outside that boundary.
Keep repeating that duplicates are impossible everywhere.
Refuse to discuss retries at all.
Answer with only broker marketing terms.
questions.explanation: Precision and honesty are stronger than absolute claims.
